title: Assessment of the Drug–Drug Interaction Potential of Lotiglipron (PF‐07081532) with Midazolam, Omeprazole, Dabigatran, Rosuvastatin, and the Oral Contraceptives Levonorgestrel and Ethinyl Estradiol
text:
[DOCUMENT METADATA]
Title: Assessment of the Drug–Drug Interaction Potential of Lotiglipron (PF‐07081532) with Midazolam, Omeprazole, Dabigatran, Rosuvastatin, and the Oral Contraceptives Levonorgestrel and Ethinyl Estradiol
Year: 2026
Type: research_article
Drugs: metformin, semaglutide, lotiglipron
Keywords: drug–drug interactions, glucagon‐like peptide‐1 receptor agonist, lotiglipron, Phase 1
Domain: Type 2 Diabetes Drug Interactions
[END METADATA]

[SECTION: ABSTRACT]
Abstract Two Phase 1 open‐label studies were conducted in otherwise healthy participants, with BMI classified as overweight/obesity, to assess the potential of lotiglipron (a glucagon‐like peptide‐1 receptor agonist) as precipitant for pharmacokinetic (PK) drug–drug interactions (DDIs) when co‐administered with substrates of cytochrome P450 (CYP) 3A (midazolam and oral contraceptive containing levonorgestrel/ethinyl estradiol [LE/EE]) and CYP2C19 (omeprazole) enzymes, and substrates of p‐glycoprotein (dabigatran), breast cancer resistance protein and organic anion transporting polypeptide transporters (rosuvastatin). Potential PK DDI between semaglutide and midazolam was also assessed. Co‐administration with lotiglipron led to modest dose‐dependent decreases in midazolam area under the concentration–time curve (AUC) (up to 30%), no clinically relevant changes in exposures of oral contraceptive (LE/EE), substantial reductions in omeprazole AUC (up to 70%) and clinically relevant increases in rosuvastatin AUC (up to 2.3‐fold). Upon co‐administration with lotiglipron, maximum observed plasma concentration (C max ) of dabigatran decreased (up to 55%) with no significant changes in AUC. No significant changes were observed in midazolam exposure (AUC inf  and C max ) following co‐administration with semaglutide. Across both studies a subset of lotiglipron‐treated participants (31% and 19% in Studies 1 and 2, respectively) had elevations in liver transaminases (alanine/aspartate aminotransferase >3 × ULN), which (based on PK data) were associated with reduced ability of the liver to transport/metabolize drugs cleared by multiple pathways. Based on the results of the two Phase 1 studies reported here along with the results of a Phase 2 study where transaminases elevations were also noted, both Phase 1 studies were prematurely terminated along with the clinical development of lotiglipron. 
 ClinicalTrials.gov identifiers :  NCT05671653 ;  NCT05788328 .

[SECTION: INTRODUCTION]
Approved peptidic glucagon‐like peptide‐1 receptor (GLP‐1R) agonists have been proven to be effective treatments for glycemic control, chronic weight management, and improvement of cardiovascular outcomes. 
 1 
 ,  
 2 
 ,  
 3 
 ,  
 4 
 ,  
 5 
 ,  
 6 
  However, the need for subcutaneous administration of the approved peptidic GLP‐1R agonists (or strict fasting requirements for the oral formulation of semaglutide [Rybelsus] 
 7 
 ) represent potential barriers to patients. As an alternative, lotiglipron is a selective, potent small‐molecule GLP‐1R agonist that can be administered orally once daily (QD) without fasting requirements.
In a Phase 1 study, two cohorts of participants received single lotiglipron doses of 100 and 200 mg in the fasted state and with a high‐fat breakfast in separate study periods. The pharmacokinetic (PK) results from this study indicated no meaningful impact of food on lotiglipron exposure and thus lotiglipron may be administered without regard to food. Following multiple‐dose administration of lotiglipron to patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) or obesity, maximum observed plasma concentration (C max ) was observed at 2 to 8 h. Mean half‐life ranged from 20.70 to 26.50 h, while apparent oral clearance (CL/F) and apparent volume of distribution (V z /F) ranged from 0.34 to 0.45 L/h and 11.97 to 15.65 L, respectively, over the dose range studied (10 to 180 mg QD). Lotiglipron exposure (C max  and area under the concentration–time curve from time 0 to 24 h [AUC 24 ]) generally increased in an approximately dose‐proportional manner across the dose range studied and accumulation of less than 2.1‐fold was observed with QD dosing. 
 8
In two Phase 1, placebo‐controlled, multiple‐dose studies in adults with T2D inadequately controlled on metformin and in adults with obesity without diabetes, lotiglipron demonstrated dose‐dependent reductions in glycemic indices in participants with T2D and in body weight in those with obesity. 
 8 
  Promising efficacy data indicating clinically meaningful HbA1c and body weight reductions were also reported in a Phase 2 randomized study in participants with T2D or obesity. 
 9
However, on June 25, 2023, the clinical development of lotiglipron was terminated based on emerging clinical data in the Phase 2 study ( NCT05579977 ; C3991004) 
 9 
  and in two Phase 1 drug–drug interaction (DDI) studies (described in this manuscript), in which laboratory measurements of elevated liver transaminases were observed in a subset of participants. None of these participants with alanine aminotransferase/aspartate aminotransferase (ALT/AST)>3 × upper limit of normal (ULN) reported liver‐related symptoms or adverse effects, there was no evidence of liver failure, and none needed treatment. 
 10
In vitro studies have shown that lotiglipron is a reversible and time‐dependent inhibitor of cytochrome P450 (CYP) 3A and CYP2C19. In addition, in vitro data indicated that lotiglipron has the potential to inhibit a number of drug transporters including p‐glycoprotein (P‐gp), breast cancer resistance protein (BCRP), organic anion transporting polypeptide (OATP) 1B1, and OATP1B3.
Here, we report the results of the two Phase 1 DDI studies, designed to investigate the potential of lotiglipron as precipitant for PK interactions when co‐administered with drugs that are substrates of these enzymes and transporters. The first study ( NCT05671653 ; C3991040; hereafter referred to as Study 1) assessed the effect of lotiglipron administration on the PK of midazolam, a sensitive index substrate of CYP3A; omeprazole, a sensitive index substrate of CYP2C19; and a progestin/estrogen‐based combined oral contraceptive, the components of which are substrates of CYP3A and other metabolic enzymes (Cohort 1). 
 11 
 ,  
 12 
 ,  
 13 
 ,  
 14 
  Cohort 2 of this study also investigated the effect of semaglutide administration on the PK of midazolam, in order to be able to contextualize whether any potentially identified impact of lotiglipron on CYP3A metabolism is unique for this compound and drug specific or whether it is related to the broader GLP‐1R mechanism of action (e.g., due to weight loss and decrease in inflammatory status). Given the in vitro inhibitory effects of lotiglipron on drug transporters, the second study ( NCT05788328 ; C3991047; hereafter referred to as Study 2) assessed the effect of lotiglipron on the PK of dabigatran, a substrate of the P‐gp transporter, and rosuvastatin, a substrate of the BCRP and OATP transporters. 
 13

Ethics:
These two Phase 1, open‐label studies were conducted at sites in Anaheim, CA (Study 1) and South Miami, FL (Study 2) in compliance with the ethical principles of the Declaration of Helsinki and in compliance with all International Conference on Harmonization Good Clinical Practices guidelines. The final protocols and informed consent documentation for the two studies were reviewed and approved by the institutional review board/independent ethics committee at the investigational center of each site.

Study Designs:
Participants in Study 1 were enrolled in either Cohort 1 (lotiglipron) or Cohort 2 (semaglutide/Wegovy). Cohort 1 participated in a nine‐period, fixed‐sequence study designed to evaluate the effect of two steady‐state dose levels of lotiglipron on the single‐dose PK of midazolam and omeprazole (delayed release tablet), administered simultaneously, and the oral contraceptive levonorgestrel/ethinyl estradiol (LE/EE) (Figure  1 ). The lack of PK interaction between midazolam and omeprazole when administered concurrently allows the evaluation of two DDIs within one study period. 
 15 
 ,  
 16 
  Cohort 2 participated in a four‐period, fixed‐sequence design to evaluate the effect of semaglutide (administered subcutaneously once per week) on the single‐dose PK of orally administered midazolam (Figure  1 ). In Cohort 1, lotiglipron was administered within 10 minutes of completion of breakfast, and midazolam, omeprazole, or the oral contraceptive were administered within 5 minutes of lotiglipron during the co‐administration periods. In Cohort 2, semaglutide and midazolam were administered within approximately 10 minutes of completion of the morning meal, with midazolam administered within 5 minutes of semaglutide during the co‐administration period.
Study 2 was an eight‐period, fixed‐sequence study designed to evaluate the effect of lotiglipron administration on the single‐dose PK of dabigatran (administered as dabigatran etexilate [DE]) and rosuvastatin (Figure  1 ). Lotiglipron was administered within 10 minutes of completion of breakfast. During the co‐administration periods, rosuvastatin was taken approximately 10 minutes after lotiglipron, while DE was taken approximately 60 minutes after lotiglipron (to maximize potential for intestinal P‐gp modulation 
 17 
 ).
Although lotiglipron can be administered without regard to food based on available PK data, in both these DDI studies lotiglipron was administered every day in the fed state with a standard breakfast to standardize dosing conditions while avoiding unnecessary fasting requirements that can be burdensome for participants.
Dosing schedules for both studies are described in detail in Tables  S1  and  S2 . Lotiglipron titration was slower in Study 1 compared to Study 2 (depicted with grey shading in Figure  1 ), as titration in Study 2 aimed to minimize participant burden associated with a long inpatient duration, while still providing a sufficient tolerability profile. In both studies, serial blood samples were collected to assess the PK of the studied drugs and selected metabolites, along with endogenous biomarkers of CYP3A induction (4β‐hydroxy‐cholesterol/cholesterol [4β‐HC/C], Study 1) and OATP activity (Coproporphyrin‐I [CP‐I], Study 2). Adverse events and safety laboratory tests were also assessed throughout the study conduct. Except where otherwise specified, all periods of Studies 1 (Cohort 1) and 2 were conducted in the inpatient setting. Study endpoints are described in Table  S3 .
These two studies, along with a Phase 2 study being conducted in parallel, were prematurely terminated by the sponsor based on the observed PK data and elevated transaminases in these three studies. 
 9 
  In Study 1, five participants discontinued study drug in Cohort 1 and in Study 2, eight of the 24 planned participants were not dosed due to premature termination of the clinical development of lotiglipron.

Participants:
Participants in Study 1 were otherwise healthy females ≥18 years of age with a body mass index (BMI) of 30.0‐45.4 kg/m 2  at screening. Participants were excluded if they had known intolerance or hypersensitivity to any of the study drugs. In Study 2, participants were otherwise healthy males or females ≥18 years of age with a BMI of ≥25 kg/m 2  at screening.
In both studies, participants who had received a GLP‐1R agonist within 90 days prior to first dose of study intervention were excluded. Women could be of child‐bearing potential but could not be pregnant, breastfeeding, or planning to become pregnant while participating in the study, and could not be on either oral contraceptives or non‐oral hormonal contraceptives prior to entry.

PK Sample Collection and Analyses:
In Study 1, blood samples were collected for the measurement of plasma concentrations of midazolam, 1‐hydroxy‐midazolam, omeprazole, 5‐hydroxy‐omeprazole, LE, EE, lotiglipron, semaglutide, and 4β‐HC/C. In Cohort 1, samples for the assessment of midazolam, omeprazole, and their respective metabolites were collected on Day 1 of Periods 1, 4, 7, and 9 (midazolam only) at 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14, and 24 h postdose. LE/EE PK samples were collected on Day 1 of Periods 2, 5, and 8 at 0, 0.75, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24, 48, 72, 96, and 120 h postdose. Lotiglipron PK samples were collected on Day 28 of Period 3 and Day 63 of Period 6 at 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14, and 24 h postdose. Samples for 4‐β‐HC/C were collected predose on Day 1 of Periods 1, 4, and 7. In Cohort 2, semaglutide PK samples were collected in Weeks 5, 9, 13, and 17 of Period 2 and prior to dosing on Day 1 of Period 3. Midazolam and 1‐hydroxy‐midazolam PK samples were collected on Day 1 of Periods 1, 3, and 4 at 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14, and 24 h postdose.
In Study 2, blood samples were collected for the measurement of plasma concentrations of dabigatran, rosuvastatin, and lotiglipron. Dabigatran PK samples were collected on Day 1 of Periods 1, 4, and 7 at 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24, and 48 h postdose. Rosuvastatin PK samples were collected on Day 1 of Periods 2, 5, and 8 at 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 14, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h postdose. Residual rosuvastatin samples were also utilized for the analysis of plasma CP‐I concentrations. Lotiglipron PK samples were collected on Day 5 of Period 4 and on Day 2 of Period 8 at 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14, and 24 h postdose.
Plasma concentrations of all analytes were analyzed using validated analytical methods in compliance with applicable SOPs. Further details on analytical methods used are provided in  Supplemental Information .

PK Data Analysis:
PK parameters were calculated using standard noncompartmental analysis methodology with an internally validated software system (oNCA, version 2.7.8). PK parameters calculated for each analyte included AUC from time 0 extrapolated to infinity (AUC inf ), AUC from time 0 to time of last measurable concentration (AUC last ), C max , time to maximum plasma concentration (T max ), terminal half‐life (t ½ ), CL/F, V z /F, and metabolite to parent ratio for AUC (MRAUC) and C max  (MRC max ), as applicable.

Statistical Analyses:
In Study 1, enrollment of ∼16 participants in each cohort was considered sufficient to ensure ∼12 participants would complete each cohort of the study. In Study 2, enrollment of ∼24 participants was considered sufficient to ensure ∼16 participants would complete the study.
Natural log e ‐transformed AUC inf , AUC last , and C max  for midazolam, omeprazole, LE, EE, dabigatran, or rosuvastatin, administered with or without lotiglipron, were analyzed using separate mixed‐effect models, with treatment as a fixed effect and participant as a random effect. Estimates of the adjusted mean differences (Test − Reference) and corresponding 90% confidence intervals (CI) were obtained from the models. The adjusted mean differences and 90% CIs for the differences were exponentiated to provide estimates of the ratio of adjusted geometric means (Test/Reference) and 90% CIs for the ratios.

Participant Disposition and Baseline Characteristics:
Sixteen female participants were assigned to the study drug in Cohort 1 of Study 1: 7 (43.8%) completed dosing in all 9 periods. Nine (56.3%) participants were White and 5 (31.3%) were Black or African American (Table  1 ). Five (31.3%) participants were Hispanic or Latino. The mean (SD) age and BMI of participants were 50.8 (13.5) years and 33.3 (2.7) kg/m 2 , respectively. Cohort 2 included 16 female participants, with 10 (62.5%) completing all four dosing periods. Eight (50.0%) participants were White and 7 (43.8%) were Black or African American (Table  1 ). Six (37.5%) were Hispanic or Latino. The mean (SD) age and BMI of participants were 48.1 (13.7) years and 34.3 (2.7) kg/m 2 , respectively.
In Study 2, 16 participants were assigned to the study drug, 14 of whom completed dosing in all 8 periods. Eleven (68.8%) participants were female and 14 (87.5%) were White (Table  1 ). All participants were Hispanic or Latino. The mean (SD) age and BMI of participants were 42.0 (11.3) years and 31.6 (3.5) kg/m 2 , respectively.

Pharmacokinetic Results:
An overview of the ratios (and 90% CI) for AUC and C max  of each drug (midazolam, omeprazole, LE/EE, dabigatran, and rosuvastatin) when co‐administered with two different dose levels of lotiglipron versus administration of the drug alone is presented in Figure  2 . The effect of lotiglipron 260 mg on the AUC of LE is not depicted due to substantial uncertainty around the effect estimate.

Effect of Lotiglipron on the PK of Midazolam and 4β‐HC/C Ratio (Study 1 Cohort 1):
Dose‐dependent decreases in midazolam AUC inf  were observed when midazolam was co‐administered with lotiglipron; however, no changes in C max  were observed (Table  2 ). During co‐administration with 80 mg QD (Period 4) and 260 mg QD (Period 7) of lotiglipron, midazolam AUC inf  was decreased by approximately 11% and 30%, respectively, compared with Period 1 when midazolam was administered without lotiglipron (Table  2 ). Following a 2‐week washout of lotiglipron (Period 9), midazolam exposures were similar to Period 1. Midazolam plasma concentration–time profiles after administration with and without lotiglipron are presented in Figure  3a . Median T max  values of midazolam ranged between 0.50 to 1.50 h across the different dosing periods and mean t ½  ranged from 6.66 to 7.54 h (Table  2 ). However, the CL/F of midazolam increased in a dose‐dependent manner following co‐administration with lotiglipron compared with Period 1 (midazolam without lotiglipron) and returned to baseline value following a 2‐week washout of lotiglipron in Period 9.
Plasma PK parameters of 1‐hydroxy‐midazolam with and without lotiglipron are summarized in Table  S4 , and corresponding plasma concentration–time profiles are presented in Figure  S1A . In general, exposures of 1‐hydroxy‐midazolam were either similar or slightly increased during co‐administration with lotiglipron. Increases in metabolite/parent exposure ratios were observed during co‐administration with lotiglipron.
Dose‐dependent increases in median plasma 4β‐hydroxy‐cholesterol/cholesterol ratios were observed with administration of lotiglipron 80 mg QD (35% increase, Period 4) and lotiglipron 260 mg QD (89% increase, Period 7), relative to baseline (Table  S5 ).

Effect of Lotiglipron on the PK of Omeprazole (Study 1 Cohort 1):
The PK of omeprazole 20 mg were highly variable both with and without lotiglipron, but particularly during lotiglipron co‐administration (Periods 4 and 7) (Table  2 ). Decreases in omeprazole exposures were observed when omeprazole was co‐administered with lotiglipron (Table  2 , Figure  3b ). The AUC last  and C max  of omeprazole decreased by 43% and 67%, respectively, during co‐administration with lotiglipron 80 mg QD, and by 70% and 36% (but with a wide confidence interval including no effect), respectively, during co‐administration with lotiglipron 260 mg QD, compared with Period 1 (omeprazole without lotiglipron). Median T max  of omeprazole was delayed by approximately 2.3 to 5.1 h when co‐administered with lotiglipron (Table  2 ); mean t ½  remained unchanged with values ranging from 1.71 to 1.80 h across different periods. Of note, t ½  was not reportable for >50% of participants during co‐administration with lotiglipron 80 or 260 mg QD. Plasma PK parameters of 5‐hydroxy‐omeprazole with and without lotiglipron are summarized in Table  S4 , and corresponding plasma concentration–time profiles are presented in Figure  S1B . Overall, 5‐hydroxy‐omeprazole exposures were lower during co‐administration of omeprazole with lotiglipron, and T max  was delayed. However, as omeprazole exposures decreased to a greater extent than those of 5‐hydroxy‐omeprazole during co‐administration with lotiglipron, increases in metabolite/parent exposure ratios were noted.

Effect of Lotiglipron on the PK of LE and EE (Study 1 Cohort 1):
Following co‐administration of LE/EE with lotiglipron (80 and 260 mg QD), dose‐dependent increases in LE AUC inf  and minor decreases in LE C max  were observed (Table  3 ). The AUC inf  of LE increased by 32% while C max  decreased by 21% during co‐administration with lotiglipron 80 mg QD compared with administration of LE/EE alone. High uncertainty was noted around the estimate of lotiglipron effect at the 260 mg QD dose level due to the small number of participants (N = 5) with LE AUC inf  values at this dose level.
Plasma concentration–time profiles of LE with and without lotiglipron are presented in Figure  3c . Median T max  of LE was 2.00 h when LE/EE was administered alone and 3.02 and 4.10 h when administered with lotiglipron 80 mg QD or 260 mg QD, respectively (Table  3 ). Mean t ½  for LE was generally similar with and without lotiglipron co‐administration and ranged from 41.26 to 45.25 h across dosing Periods 2, 5, and 8 (Table  3 ). However, CL/F and V z /F of LE were higher when LE/EE was administered alone compared with lotiglipron co‐administration.
While small decreases in EE C max  (up to 28%) were observed when LE/EE was co‐administered with lotiglipron, there was no apparent change in EE AUC inf  (Table  3 ). Plasma concentration–time profiles of EE after administration with and without lotiglipron are presented in Figure  3d . Median T max  was 2.00 h both with and without lotiglipron co‐administration. Mean t ½  for EE ranged from 22.03 to 25.54 h and CL/F ranged from 235.3 to 256.9 L/h across administration both with and without lotiglipron.

Effect of Semaglutide on the PK of Midazolam (Study 1 Cohort 2):
No differences in midazolam AUC inf  or C max  were observed when midazolam was co‐administered with semaglutide versus administration alone (Table  S6 ). Following a 2‐week washout of semaglutide (Period 4), midazolam exposures were similar to Period 1. Median T max  values of midazolam were 1.0 h across the different dosing periods and mean t ½  ranged from 6.13 to 7.53 h (Table  S6 ).
Plasma PK parameters of 1‐hydroxy‐midazolam with and without semaglutide are summarized in Table  S7 . In general, exposures of 1‐hydroxy‐midazolam were slightly increased during co‐administration with semaglutide 2.4 mg SC once weekly doses. Modest increases in metabolite/parent exposure ratios were observed during co‐administration with semaglutide. Midazolam plasma concentration–time profiles after administration with and without semaglutide are presented in Figure  S2 .

Effect of Lotiglipron on the PK of Dabigatran (Study 2):
The C max  for dabigatran (total) was significantly decreased (up to 55%) during co‐administration of DE with lotiglipron; however, only minor decreases (up to 17% and with confidence intervals including no effect) were noted in AUC inf  (Table  4 ). Dabigatran median T max  was 3.00 h when DE was administered alone and 6.00 h when co‐administered with lotiglipron (either dose); mean t ½  values of dabigatran were generally similar across administration with and without lotiglipron (Table  4 ). Dabigatran plasma concentration–time profiles after administration with and without lotiglipron are presented in Figure  4a .

Effect of Lotiglipron on the PK of Rosuvastatin and CP‐I (Study 2):
Rosuvastatin plasma exposures were higher during co‐administration with lotiglipron compared with administration alone (Table  4 , Figure  4b ). Rosuvastatin AUC inf  and C max  increased by 2.3‐fold and 1.8‐fold, respectively, following co‐administration with lotiglipron 80 mg QD and by 2.1‐fold and 2.2‐fold, respectively, following co‐administration with lotiglipron 240 mg QD, compared with rosuvastatin alone. The median T max  of rosuvastatin was 5.00 h and t ½  values were generally similar across administration both with and without lotiglipron (Table  4 ).
Plasma CP‐I C max  and AUC last  were generally similar following administration of 10 mg rosuvastatin alone and when co‐administered with 80 mg QD of lotiglipron, while modest increases of 40% and 28%, respectively, were noted at the 240 mg QD dose level of lotiglipron (Table  S8 ).

PK of Lotiglipron (Study 1 and Study 2):
In Study 1, median T max  of lotiglipron was 6.00 h at both 80 and 260 mg QD doses (Table  S9 ). Lotiglipron exposures increased in a slightly greater than dose‐proportional manner across the 80 to 260 mg QD dose range: the geometric mean AUC 24  and C max  ratios of lotiglipron between the two dose levels were 4.32‐ and 3.96‐fold, respectively, with a 3.25‐fold increase in dose. This slightly greater than dose‐proportional increase in exposure was largely driven by participants with ALT/AST elevations (discussed below). In Study 2, median T max  of lotiglipron was 6.00 h for the 80 mg QD dose and 4.00 h for the 240 mg QD dose (Table  S9 ). Lotiglipron exposures increased in a generally dose‐proportional manner over the 3‐fold dose range. Overall, the available lotiglipron PK data in these DDI studies from participants without ALT/AST elevations were generally consistent with that observed in a prior Phase 1 multiple‐dose study. 
 8

PK in Participants with ALT/AST Elevations:
A total of 5 of 16 (31%, in Study 1 Cohort 1) and 3 of 16 (19%, in Study 2) participants, were observed to have ALT/AST elevations >3 × ULN.
In Study 1 (Cohort 1), the elevations in these five participants occurred at or later than study Day 55 and therefore were absent during the PK assessments at baseline and at 80 mg QD of lotiglipron, but potentially impacted the PK assessments at the lotiglipron dose of 260 mg QD (Figure  S3 ). For these five participants, midazolam, omeprazole, LE, and EE, as well as lotiglipron exposures assessed before the occurrence of the elevations, were generally consistent with those observed in participants without ALT/AST elevations (Tables  S10–S14 ).
Three of these five participants contributed to the midazolam DDI assessment with lotiglipron 260 mg QD (Period 7) and had the highest midazolam AUC inf  values and AUC inf  ratios (compared with baseline) among the eight participants with PK data (Table  S10 ). The AUC inf  ratio was >1 in two of the three participants (indicating potential inhibition of CYP3A metabolism), while participants without ALT/AST elevations had AUC inf  ratios that ranged from 0.40 to 0.63 (indicating induction of CYP3A metabolism).
Three of the five participants contributed to the omeprazole DDI assessment with lotiglipron 260 mg QD (Period 7), with two of these three participants having ALT/AST >3 × ULN at that time. These two participants had the highest omeprazole C max  values and C max  ratios (compared with baseline) among the eight participants with PK data. Both participants exhibited a C max  ratio >1 (2.58 and 14.83), while participants without ALT/AST elevations had C max  ratios ranging from 0.15 to 0.41, suggesting potential impairment of CYP2C19 metabolism in the participants with ALT/AST elevations (Table  S11 ).
One of the five participants had available LE exposure data in the DDI assessment with lotiglipron 260 mg QD (Period 8). This participant had the highest LE AUC last  value, and the highest C max  and AUC last  ratios (compared with baseline) among the six participants with PK data in Period 8 (Table  S12 ). Two of the five participants had available EE exposure data in the DDI assessment with lotiglipron 260 mg QD. One of these two participants had the highest EE AUC last  value, while both had the highest AUC last  ratios (compared with baseline) among the seven participants with PK data in Period 8 (Table  S13 ). These results indicate that ALT/AST elevations may be associated with potential impairment in LE/EE metabolism.
Three of the five participants with ALT/AST elevations had lotiglipron PK assessed at 260 mg QD (Period 6; following titration from 100 mg QD) and had the highest lotiglipron exposures (AUC 24  and C max ) and highest exposure ratios (260 mg compared with 80 mg) among all eight participants with PK data (Table  S14 ). For these three participants, lotiglipron AUC 24  and C max  ratios ranged from 6.39 to 8.53 and from 5.81 to 7.10, respectively (for a 3.25× increase in dose), indicating greater than dose‐proportional increases in exposure. In contrast, in participants without ALT/AST elevations, approximately dose‐proportional increases in exposure were observed. These data suggest that ALT/AST elevations are associated with potential impairment of OATP uptake and/or CYP3A metabolism, main pathways for lotiglipron clearance.
In Study 2, 3 of the 16 (19%) participants were observed to have ALT/AST elevations >3 × ULN. These elevations occurred at or later than study Day 40 and therefore were absent during the PK assessments at baseline and at lotiglipron 80 mg QD, but potentially impacted the PK assessments at lotiglipron 240 mg QD (Figure  S4 ).
For the three participants who experienced ALT/AST elevations during the study, dabigatran (total) exposures were generally consistent with participants without ALT/AST elevations, regardless of whether dabigatran was administered alone (baseline) or co‐administered with lotiglipron (80 or 240 mg QD) (Table  S15 ). Rosuvastatin exposures (AUC last ) at baseline for these three participants were generally consistent with values observed for participants without ALT/AST elevations. However, two of the three participants with ALT/AST >3 × ULN exhibited the highest rosuvastatin AUC last  ratios (vs baseline) at lotiglipron 80 mg QD, while at 240 mg QD, three of the four highest AUC last  ratios were exhibited by the participants with ALT/AST >3 × ULN, suggesting potential impairment in OATP or BCRP transport (Table  S16 ).

Safety and Tolerability:
In Cohort 1 of Study 1, 115 treatment‐emergent AEs were reported in 16 (100%) participants. No serious adverse events (SAEs), discontinuation from study due to AEs, or deaths were reported (Table  S17 ). Three (18.8%) participants discontinued from study drug due to AEs, two in Period 6 and one in Period 7. One participant experienced a severe AE of nausea in Period 6. Among the 91 AEs with ≥2 occurrences, 60, 30, and 1 were assessed to be of mild, moderate, and severe intensity, respectively. The most frequently reported AEs with ≥2 occurrences were nausea (100%), constipation (93.8%), decreased appetite (62.5%), dyspepsia (56.3%), and headache (56.3%). Five participants (31.3%) were noted to have ALT >3 × ULN and AST >3 × ULN, all of which were reported starting in Period 6 after at least 7 weeks of lotiglipron dosing and at doses ≥120 mg QD. Return to baseline took up to 8 weeks post last dose of lotiglipron. One participant discontinued the study drug due to an AE (listed as AST increased) in Period 6, at which time laboratory results were notable for elevated ALT (9.5 × ULN) and AST (4.3 × ULN), after a peak level of ALT (13.7 × ULN) and AST (6.5 × ULN).
In Cohort 2 of Study 1, 55 all‐causality TEAEs were reported in 14 (87.5%) participants. No SAE, severe AE, discontinuation from study due to AEs, or deaths were reported. Two (12.5%) participants discontinued from study drug due to AEs, one in Period 2 and one in Period 3. Among 36 AEs with ≥2 occurrences, 31 and 5 were assessed to be mild and moderative severity, respectively. The most frequently reported AEs with ≥2 occurrences were somnolence (68.8%), constipation (31.3%), headache (25.0%), dizziness (18.8%), and nausea (18.8%).
In Study 2, 91 AEs were reported in 16 (100%) participants after first dose in Period 1. There were no deaths or discontinuations from the study due to AEs (Table  S17 ). Among the 91 AEs, 69, 19, and 3 were assessed to be of mild, moderate, and severe intensity, respectively. The most frequently reported AEs were dyspepsia (75.0%), diarrhea (68.8%), and nausea, constipation, and vomiting (each reported by 62.5% of participants). Three participants (18.8%) had both ALT >3 × ULN and AST >3 × ULN by Period 8 and at doses ≥220 mg QD, with the first occurring in Period 6 after ∼5 weeks of lotiglipron dosing, and with all returning to baseline up to 15 weeks post last dose of lotiglipron. In one participant, AE of elevated ALT/AST in Period 8 led to permanent discontinuation of lotiglipron. The participant continued in the study; however, this AE, while asymptomatic, was upgraded to an SAE when the ALT elevations reached >20 × ULN accompanied with AST >5 × ULN.
None of the participants with ALT/AST elevations in either study reported any liver‐related symptoms/adverse effects, there was no evidence of liver failure, and none needed treatment. No meaningful changes in alkaline phosphatase or total bilirubin were observed.

[SECTION: DISCUSSION]
The two Phase 1 studies reported here assessed the potential of lotiglipron as precipitant of PK DDIs when co‐administered with substrates of CYP3A and CYP2C19 enzymes, substrates of P‐gp, BCRP and OATP transporters, and an oral contraceptive.
In Study 1, midazolam exposure (AUC inf ) decreased by up to 30% when co‐administered with lotiglipron, then returned to baseline levels following a 2‐week washout of lotiglipron. In addition, the metabolite/parent ratio for 1‐hydroxy‐midazolam was increased during co‐administration with lotiglipron, as well as the 4β‐HC/C plasma ratio (a well‐studied endogenous biomarker to assess potential for induction of CYP3A‐mediated metabolism 
 18 
 ,  
 19 
 ). The collective data indicate lotiglipron is a weak inducer of CYP3A. In contrast, in vitro data for lotiglipron noted CYP3A inhibition with no apparent induction potential. Such a disconnect between in vitro and clinical DDI data has been observed with at least one other molecule with GLP‐1R activity, where CYP3A down‐regulation was observed in vitro, while clinical DDI data with midazolam showed no effect on AUC and a reduction in C max . 
 20
Obesity and weight loss have also been shown to impact CYP3A activity. Obesity has long been associated with a state of chronic, low‐level inflammation, 
 21 
 ,  
 22 
 ,  
 23 
  with adipose tissue serving as a primary source of inflammatory mediators, including IL‐6. 
 24 
  Inflammatory cytokines, such as IL‐6, IL‐1β, and TNFα have been shown to inhibit CYP3A activity 
 25 
 ,  
 26 
  and prior publications have demonstrated lower clearance of CYP3A substrates, including midazolam, in participants with obesity. 
 27 
 ,  
 28 
  Decreases in IL‐6 and other inflammatory mediators have been observed following weight loss, 
 29 
 ,  
 30 
 ,  
 31 
  and a number of clinical studies have demonstrated increased clearance of CYP3A substrates following weight loss, presumably due to increased CYP3A activity. 
 32 
 ,  
 33 
 ,  
 34
The objective of including Cohort 2 in Study 1 (impact of semaglutide on midazolam PK) was to test the hypothesis that decreases in body weight lessen the inflammatory burden of obesity and attenuate the inhibitory impact of inflammatory mediators on CYP3A activity. The subsequent “normalization” of CYP3A activity would then manifest as a reduction in midazolam exposure. As semaglutide is a peptide, CYP‐mediated DDIs were not expected, and in vitro studies have shown very low potential for semaglutide to inhibit or induce CYP enzymes. 
 35 
 ,  
 36 
  Semaglutide, as administered in the current study, was expected to result in significant weight loss, therefore, any decrease in midazolam exposure could be attributed to mechanism‐based origins; that is, weight loss, decrease in inflammatory status, reduced levels of inflammatory mediators, and so on.
Significant decreases in body weight were observed over time for both Study 1 cohorts, with median (range) percent change from baseline of −8.55 (−13.0, −7.1) kg by Period 9 Day 1 (125 days total) for Cohort 1 (lotiglipron) and −13.15 (−22.0, −5.9) kg by Period 4 Day 1 (165 days total) for Cohort 2 (semaglutide). As discussed above, in Cohort 1 midazolam exposures decreased during lotiglipron co‐administration but returned to baseline levels following a 2‐week washout of lotiglipron. If the decrease in midazolam exposure was solely due to weight loss and subsequent normalization of CYP3A activity, midazolam exposures would be expected to remain decreased even after lotiglipron was discontinued. In addition, in Cohort 2, there were no significant changes in midazolam exposures following co‐administration with semaglutide 2.4 mg SC QW or at 2 weeks following discontinuation of semaglutide. Thus, results from this study fail to provide direct and conclusive evidence of a GLP‐1R mechanism‐related effect on CYP3A activity that manifests as CYP3A induction (e.g., via weight loss, reduction of inflammatory burden, and so on), and suggest that the changes in midazolam exposure observed in Cohort 1 can most likely be attributed to weak CYP3A induction by lotiglipron via an unknown but not GLP‐1R agonism‐related mechanism.
Omeprazole exposure was decreased following co‐administration with lotiglipron. This result contrasts with expectations based on in vitro data, where lotiglipron was identified to be a time‐dependent CYP2C19 inhibitor. The decreased omeprazole exposure observed clinically is hypothesized to be driven by lotiglipron's impact on the absorption of omeprazole. GLP‐1R agonists like lotiglipron are known to delay gastric emptying. Given that omeprazole is an acid labile drug, 
 37 
  delayed gastric emptying may lead to increased degradation of omeprazole resulting in less drug available for absorption. 
 20 
  This hypothesis is further supported by the observed 2.3 to 5.1 h delay in omeprazole T max  upon co‐administration with lotiglipron, and a previous observation of a similarly substantial decrease in omeprazole exposure during co‐administration of another molecule with GLP‐1R activity. 
 20
Modest or no changes were observed in LE and EE exposures following co‐administration with lotiglipron, providing support that lotiglipron is not anticipated to cause a clinically relevant interaction upon co‐administration with oral contraceptives. However, due to the small number of participants completing the DDI assessment at the high dose of lotiglipron (260 mg QD), there is high uncertainty in the magnitude of DDI effects at this dose. In addition, the impact of lotiglipron on the exposures of EE/LE after the first dose of a newly escalated dose level is unknown. It is acknowledged that other GLP‐1R agonists (peptides) have demonstrated a greater degree of gastric emptying following the first dose compared with that at steady state following multiple doses, an effect which has been attributed to tachyphylaxis. 
 38 
  However, given that lotiglipron is a small‐molecule GLP‐1R agonist with additional DDI liabilities (e.g., impact on CYP3A metabolism) apart from the gastric emptying effect, assessment of the DDI effect at a time that the maximum steady‐state exposure has been achieved was deemed the most appropriate.
In Study 2, co‐administration with lotiglipron significantly decreased C max  of dabigatran and delayed its T max , with no substantial impact on AUC. These results are indicative of a decrease in the absorption rate of dabigatran most likely due to a delay in gastric emptying attributable to lotiglipron co‐administration, rather than an increase in the extent of dabigatran intestinal absorption as would be expected via P‐gp inhibition.
Rosuvastatin exposures were increased by approximately 2‐fold when co‐administered with lotiglipron, with no clear dose‐dependency noted between the 80 and 240 mg QD doses of lotiglipron. Since rosuvastatin is a known substrate of both BCRP and OATP1B transporters and because lotiglipron inhibits both transporters in vitro, plasma CP‐I levels (an endogenous biomarker of OATP1B activity 
 39 
 ) were measured to delineate the underlying mechanism of the rosuvastatin interaction. Given that there were no changes in CP‐I exposures at lotiglipron 80 mg QD and only modest increases in CP‐I exposures at lotiglipron 240 mg QD, it can be inferred that the observed interaction between lotiglipron and rosuvastatin is primarily driven by BCRP inhibition with minimal OATP contribution.
In total, 31% (in Study 1 Cohort 1) and 19% (in Study 2) of study participants were observed to have ALT/AST >3 × ULN at doses ≥120 mg QD (in Study 1 Cohort 1) and at ≥220 mg QD (in Study 2), after dosing with lotiglipron for at least 7 (in Study 1 Cohort 1) and 5 (in Study 2) weeks. The timing of these elevations is consistent with the Phase 2 study (ClinicalTrials.gov:  NCT05579977 ), where approximately 6% of participants treated with lotiglipron had ALT/AST elevations >3 × ULN with the majority of these occurring following 8 weeks of treatment, though they were generally observed at lower doses (40‐80 mg QD) following a slower titration scheme. 
 9 
  It is unclear if the higher incidence of ALT/AST elevations observed in the two Phase 1 studies reported here can be directly attributed to the faster titration scheme employed as it is confounded by the fact that the early termination of the Phase 2 study minimized the number of participants reaching lotiglipron doses ≥140 mg QD.
Following the occurrence of ALT/AST >3 × ULN in a subset of participants in the two Phase 1 studies, concentrations of multiple compounds (including lotiglipron) cleared by multiple pathways were higher compared with the exposures observed in participants without such elevations. On the contrary, no substantial differences in exposures were observed between these two groups before the occurrence of the elevations. In the Phase 2 study, lotiglipron concentrations were also higher in the participants with ALT/AST elevations versus those without. 
 9 
  These data support the hypothesis that lotiglipron resulted in liver transaminase elevations in a subset of participants which was associated with reduced ability of the liver to transport/metabolize drugs cleared by multiple pathways. This was recognized as an important safety concern that contributed to the decision to terminate the clinical development of lotiglipron, especially given the inability to identify a priori the individuals at risk of transaminases elevation and hence also at risk of increased exposures of concomitant medications and potential need for dose adjustment. Characteristically, one participant in Study 2 with the highest magnitude of ALT elevation (∼35 × ULN) had an approximate 10‐fold increase in rosuvastatin exposure (vs baseline) following the occurrence of the transaminase elevations, which carries the potential for increased risk of severe and/or serious adverse consequences if encountered in the less controlled, outpatient setting.
Subsequent to the observations of elevated ALT/AST in the two Phase 1 studies, potential precipitating factors were explored. These included a review of the detailed medical histories undertaken at the screening visit and concomitant medications/dietary supplements (permitted per protocol). Additionally, a comprehensive panel of laboratory tests and hepatic imaging was completed for each individual. In all cases, the increased ALT/AST was reported as being unrelated to prior medical history, concomitant medications, or clinical trial procedures. The investigator in each study considered there was a reasonable possibility that the increased ALT/AST was related to lotiglipron, with which the sponsor concurred.
There were no fatal events in these Phase 1 studies. The majority of AEs reported across these two Phase 1 studies were mild in severity, and reported by all participants. Consistent with the data reported for other GLP‐1R agonists, 
 40 
 ,  
 41 
  the most frequently reported AEs were GI related.
The two DDI studies were limited by their premature termination that led to discontinuation of five participants in Study 1 (Cohort 1) and dosing less participants than originally planned in Study 2. However, with one exception (high uncertainty around the effect of 260 mg QD lotiglipron on LE exposures) the studies successfully met their objectives.

[SECTION: CONCLUSIONS]
The two Phase 1 studies reported here assessed the potential of lotiglipron as precipitant of PK DDIs when co‐administered with substrates of CYP3A and CYP2C19 enzymes, substrates of P‐gp, BCRP and OATP transporters, and an oral contraceptive. The key DDI liabilities of lotiglipron identified based on study results are: (1) weak CYP3A induction that can be potentially clinically relevant when co‐administered with CYP3A substrates with narrow therapeutic index; (2) clinically relevant reductions in omeprazole exposure; and (3) clinically relevant increases in the exposure of rosuvastatin and potentially other BCRP substrates. Across both studies a subset of participants had ALT/AST >3 × ULN, which based on PK data, was associated with reduced ability of the liver to transport/metabolize drugs cleared by multiple pathways. Based on the results of the two Phase 1 studies reported here, along with the results of a Phase 2 study where transaminase elevations were also noted, the clinical development of lotiglipron was terminated.
